message:
fix(health): don't flag a not-initialized install as "index stale"

`run_health(...)` produces a diagnostic list of error strings that get
surfaced in the `errors[]` array of the JSON response. One of those
checks was:

    if !index_fresh {
        errors.push("index stale".to_string());
    }

…which fires whenever the freshness check returns false — including the
legitimate "this install has never been indexed" case. A fresh install
has no stored "last indexed" timestamp at all, so `index_fresh` is
false by definition, and the health output then said:

    { "status": "not_initialized", "errors": ["index stale", ...] }

That's misleading. "Stale" implies there is an index and it is out of
date; the whole point of the `not_initialized` status is that there
isn't one. Users who read the errors[] list (dashboards, paging rules,
the `--robot` JSON consumers) would open an alert that the UI then
said was already covered by `not_initialized`.

Fix: gate the "index stale" push on both `index_exists` AND
`!not_initialized`. The predicate now reads:

    if index_exists && !index_fresh && !not_initialized {
        errors.push("index stale".to_string());
    }

Matches the intent of the check: only flag staleness when there's
actually an index to be stale. When the install truly is
not-initialized, the status field already says so and the errors[]
array no longer double-reports.

No change for installs that had an index and it genuinely was stale
(e.g., long-running unattended dev box that hasn't seen `cass index`
in a while); those still get the "index stale" error.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
